title: I got tired of spending hours on Binance API docs just to run a simple bot, so I built something
author: Ok_Security_1684
selftext:
I got tired of spending hours on Binance API docs just to run a simple bot, so I built something



You know the feeling - you have a clear strategy in your head ("open a long when ETH funding goes negative + RSI < 30") but turning that into working code takes a whole weekend. I built a tool that skips all of that. You type the strategy in plain English, it gives you a ready-to-run Python bot. Not a template - actual code, your exact conditions, paste your API key and hit run.



What it handles out of the box:

Funding rates, RSI, MACD, EMA cross, volume spikes, whale transfers

Leverage, TP/SL, position sizing (fixed USD or % of balance)

Full error handling, retry logic, cooldown - already in the code

Auto risk scoring so you know what you're getting into before you run it



→ [cryptontradebot.com/natural-language-trading.html](http://cryptontradebot.com/natural-language-trading.html) \- free to try, no account needed. Drop your strategy in the comments and I'll show you what the output looks like.

comments.author: dogazine4570
comments.body:
This is a cool idea. The friction between “I know the logic” and “now I have to wrestle with the API, websockets, auth, rate limits, error handling…” is very real.

A couple of questions that would matter a lot to me before trying it:

- How are you handling backtesting? Does it generate just live-trading code, or can it also scaffold a backtest with historical klines/funding data?
- What about risk management defaults (position sizing, max drawdown guards, stop-loss/TP logic)? Plain-English strategies often forget that part.
- How do you deal with Binance quirks like timestamp drift, recvWindow, and rate limits?
- Is the API key stored locally only, or does anything pass through your servers?

If it truly outputs readable, transparent Python (not some obfuscated wrapper), that’s a big plus. The biggest fear with auto-generated trading bots is hidden logic you don’t fully understand.

Would be interested to see a sample strategy → generated code comparison.
